turns.content: man I got a lot on my plate right now. trying to figure out what I need to do to get ready for this county inspection

County inspections can be stressful but they're manageable if you plan ahead. What type of inspection is it — fire/safety, business license renewal, environmental compliance? And when is it scheduled?
got the county inspection coming march 15 and I gotta get the fire extinguishers recertified before then. thats the big one I keep putting off

OK, March 15 gives you about seven weeks, which is plenty of time if you start now. Fire extinguisher recertification is usually the thing that trips people up because you have to schedule a third-party company to come out, and they can take a week or two to get on the books.

I'd call a fire safety company this week to get on their schedule. They'll inspect, recharge if needed, and give you new tags. Usually runs $20-50 per extinguisher depending on size and type.

Beyond the extinguishers, what else are they typically looking at in your inspection? Do you have a checklist from last time?

I think I got the checklist somewhere in my office. its a mess in there. they look at fire extinguishers, exits, electrical, waste disposal, all that stuff. last time they dinged me for not having the right signage on my oil disposal drum

Find that checklist — that's your roadmap. Everything else is guessing.

For the signage issue, that's an easy fix. You need proper hazardous waste labels on your oil drums and any other waste containers. You can buy compliant labels online for a few bucks, or your waste hauler might provide them.

Here's a general punch list for auto shop inspections:

- Fire extinguishers current and mounted properly
- Exit signs illuminated and paths clear
- Electrical panels accessible (nothing stored within 3 feet)
- Waste oil and fluid containers labeled and sealed
- Spill kit on hand
- Eye wash station if required in your county
- First aid kit stocked

Does that match what they looked at last time?
yeah thats about right. the electrical panel thing worries me, we got stuff stacked up in front of it. I know better but the shop is tight on space

That's a guaranteed ding and it's a safety issue too, so move that stuff first. The 3-foot clearance rule is one they never let slide.

If space is tight, think vertical. Shelving on the walls above the work areas can get a lot of stuff off the floor. Even a couple of heavy-duty wall-mounted shelves near the panel area would help.

I'd start with three things this week:
1. Call the fire extinguisher company to get on their schedule
2. Clear the electrical panel area
3. Find that checklist

That knocks out the biggest items and gives you five more weeks for the smaller stuff.
five weeks sounds like a lot until you factor in that im also running the shop every day. hard to do all this when youre elbow deep in transmissions

I hear you. The trick is to not try to do it all at once. Pick one item off the list per week and assign what you can to your guys.

Something like:
- This week: call the fire extinguisher company and clear the panel
- Next week: labels and signage
- Week after: exits and walkways
- Week 4: spill kit, first aid, eye wash check
- Week 5: final walkthrough, pretend you're the inspector

The final walkthrough is important. Walk through with the checklist and be picky. Anything that looks off to you will definitely look off to them.
do my own walkthrough. thats a good idea. alright what happens if I fail something, they give me time to fix it or they shut me down

Depends on the severity, but for most stuff — signage, extinguisher tags, clutter — they'll give you a notice of violation with a deadline to correct it, usually 30 days. They come back and reinspect.

They're not going to shut you down for a missing label. The stuff that can get you shut down immediately is serious electrical hazards, structural issues, or complete absence of fire suppression. Sounds like you're nowhere near that territory.

The goal is to pass clean on the first try so you don't have to deal with a reinspection. That's just more stress and sometimes a reinspection fee.
yeah I dont need that. alright man im gonna dig through that office and find the checklist tonight. appreciate the game plan

Good deal. Start with the checklist and the fire extinguisher call — those are your two highest priority items. You've got enough time to do this right if you chip away at it.

You'll be fine. Take care.
